entry.title: Writing - capitalisation
entry.summary: One of the things I'm passionate about is writing. The kind of writing, where you are able to communicate exactly what you are thinking, and exactly what you mean. It's incredibly hard. Part of writing is the words you choose, part is the formatting, and…
entry.content:
One of the things I'm passionate about is writing. The kind of writing, where you are able to communicate exactly what you are thinking, and exactly what you mean. It's incredibly hard. Part of writing is the words you choose, part is the formatting, and part is just a desire to be understood.

English is full of ambiguous words. My first appreciation for what I'm going to call 'technical writing' is when I was talking to a technical writer who worked for an aircraft company. They said they were not able to write 'close the hatch' in any maintenance manuals, since the english word 'close' means both 'close to' (aka 'near'), and well as fully closed. This was a light bulb moment for me. So the outcome of asking someone to 'close the hatch' is essentially the same as schrödinger's cat. (Some missing context: the english manuals they were writing would end up being used by people where english was not their first language, and also the documents may get translated.)

Anyway that was where I realised writing is actually a science, and you can be good at it, and you can be bad at it. By default, everyone is bad at it. But lets get good together.

To get extremely specific, extremely quickly, I want to talk about capitals.

Capitals

Not the city kind. The letter kind. THE ONES THAT MAKE YOU SHOUT. And also create and destroy meaning and brands. Marketing aside, if you've read old technical documents, you probably don't have to go far to find something written in ALL CAPS. I'm not sure why this was the case (yes), but some people have said it's because: all caps is easier to read.

I disagree.

I believe it's easiest to read writing when letters have with shape. People read with word shapes.

This can be easily proven, by the text meme that went around the web many years ago. I'll copy it below:

Aoccdrnig to a rscheearch at Cmabrigde Uinervtisy, it deosn’t mttaer in waht oredr the ltteers in a wrod are, the olny iprmoetnt tihng is taht the frist and lsat ltteer be at the rghit pclae. The rset can be a toatl mses and you can sitll raed it wouthit porbelm. Tihs is bcuseae the huamn mnid deos not raed ervey lteter by istlef, but the wrod as a wlohe.

Now there wasn't as much going on in the Internet spaces back then, well apart from geocities and neopets and other Xeo named things (SEO?). But this text meme where the letters are jumbled should make you realise word shape has meaning. Capitals destroy word shape. So I rest my case.

But don't just take my word for it, lets look at what various other people say about use of capitals.

[UK] Writing for gov.uk

When you learn to read, you start with a mix of upper and lower case but you do not start understanding uppercase until you’re around 6 years old.

At first, you may sound out letters, merge sounds, merge letters and so learn the word.

Then you stop reading it.

At that point, you recognise the shape of the word. This speeds up comprehension and speed of reading.

As writers, we do not want people to read. We want people to recognise the ‘shape’ of the word and understand. It’s a lot faster.

Capital letters are reputed to be 13 to 18% harder for users to read. So we try to avoid them.

Block capitals indicate shouting in common online usage. We are government. We should not be shouting.

This is perfect. Chef's kiss. I have nothing to add. The entire gov.uk team are world leading, and their website truly is pioneering for accessibility.

[AUS] Punctuation and capitalisation

Capitalise the first word in a sentence. Use lower case for all other words, unless those words include proper nouns. 

The guidance here is bad, but if you read it sympathetically and give them all the benefit of the doubt, as if you know what they are trying to say, you could say you understand. I mean that are telling you to capitalise the first word in a sentence. ASSUMING it's not like this. OR maybe it is? EITHER way, they probably don't mean it like this. BUT if we assume it's what we already know, then we get their gist. It's just not actually helpful and they would be better off saying nothing about it.

[AUS-QLD] Web writing and style guide

Do not use all capitals—this format is reserved for DANGER and WARNING signs

Yeah the message is getting worse as you move down the levels of government in Australia. This guidance for web writing is inside a docx file that it asks you to download. It at least mentions capitals, so good on them. Danger and warning signs somehow show up in a web writing and style guide. Not sure why, but they are interesting in general, so I don't blame the author.

There's basically the governments out of the way, there are more governments, but as an Australian, I'm focussing on rules for someone writing in Australia. I still care a lot about the writing rules in all other countries, but if trying to convince someone in Australia how it should be, then basically we have to go up the authority chain, and it essentially ends at the King of England.

Lets look at an education institution.

[US-HARVARD] Design for readability

Avoid using all caps. Readability is reduced with all caps because all words have a uniform rectangular shape, meaning readers can't identify words by their shape.

I like this one, it says what not to do, which is good. I know I'm harping on about the [AUS] government guidance, but it's just so obvious that it's basically useless to most people who are seeking writing advice (I'm refering to "Capitalise the first word in a sentence" advice still).

What about some industry bodies? If that's what W3C is, apart from a cryptic acronym that feels old, and who's websites are always a bit ugly.

[W3C]

Capitalization

Text in all capital letters is more difficult to read for most people, with and without disabilities.

Yeah this it what I was saying earlier. It's weird going back through all these references and realising I probably think the way I do because I read all of these many years ago. In any case, I'm glad the link rot hasn't set in, and all the references still return real working websites.

Oh wait I spoke to soon. I hadn't yet clicked on my last link yet. It was 

https://www.visionaustralia.org/radio/business-consulting/digital-access/blog/typography-in-inclusive-design-part-1

But yeah it's dead. I guess with a link that long it was ineviible.

Oh wait! I found it again.

https://www.visionaustralia.org/business-consulting/digital-access/blog/typography-in-inclusive-design-part-1

Limit the use of ALL CAPS text

Consider avoiding or limiting the use of ALL CAPS text.

Sigh. I notice a trend that the Australian references are very non commital. It's asking you to consider to avoid it. Or consider limiting the use of? Doesn't really mention what the limit should be set at. Either way, they clearly don't really like ALL CAPS, but they are also a bit sheepish about telling you not to.

That's ok. I can fill the void.

DON'T USE ALL CAPS.

entry.title: Making this website
entry.summary: Hi, this is my first foray into writing content for the web, using more than just a code editor, and github pages. Now it includes Publii, which is what Matt Sayar used. I hope to write about interesting things, especially on the topic of infrastructure…
entry.content:
Hi, this is my first foray into writing content for the web, using more than just a code editor, and github pages. Now it includes Publii, which is what Matt Sayar used.

I hope to write about interesting things, especially on the topic of infrastructure (or as it used to be known, public works).
